contents.title: OntoScope: Using a Divergent-Convergent Interaction Framework to Support LLM-based Ontology Scoping
contents.abstract: An ontology is a formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization that, with problem‑solving and reasoning methods, supports efficient semantic technology development. In ontology engineering, Competency Questions (CQs) capture functional requirements that define an ontology's application domain. Auditing this domain scope with CQs is challenging because in nature, there are no clear domain boundaries, and ontology engineers must then decide which subdomains to cover (horizontal coverage) and how much detail to model (vertical granularity) in an ontology. LLM‑based systems can generate many candidate CQs to guide these decisions, but current tools underuse this potential: they lack support for users' divergent (lateral) and convergent (vertical) thinking in a visualized CQs space organized by coverage and granularity. As a result, users struggle to systematically decide which CQs to adopt, discard, or refine. We propose an interaction framework that fills this gap, demonstrated through OntoScope, an LLM‑based interactive system, and a user study with 15 ontology engineers. To our knowledge, this is the first validated interaction framework with an LLM‑based system that helps ontology engineers audit domain boundaries and unifies fragmented, expert‑driven ontology scoping practices into a coherent, accessible approach. More broadly, it shows how LLM‑based systems can transparently and accountably support a wider range of knowledge‑intensive tasks.

contents.title: Embodied Digital Therapists with LLM Personalization for Aphasia Rehabilitation: Characterizing Human-AI Collaboration Boundaries
contents.abstract: Aphasia following stroke affects millions globally, yet rehabilitation remains severely limited by speech therapist shortages. Existing digital systems rely on static video demonstrations, single-modality assessment, and rule-based feedback, failing to address authentic clinical needs. Through formative investigation with five therapists, three patients, and their caregivers, we identified concrete clinical challenges: therapists spending 30-40% of time on repetitive demonstrations, existing tools providing only speech scores without articulatory evaluation, and patients struggling with complex interfaces and monotonous content. To address these challenges, we developed an integrated rehabilitation system combining an embodied digital therapist for Action Observation Therapy, tri-dimensional assessment coordinating speech quality, lip movement accuracy, and semantic understanding, and large language model-driven personalization for content generation and adaptive training. We conducted a proof-of-concept evaluation across two in-situ hospital training sessions with six patients, six caregivers, and three therapists. Results demonstrated substantial efficiency gains, with therapists spending 69-78% less time per patient. Patient acceptance improved 42.6% across sessions, and low digital literacy patients showed steepest gains (+69.0%). However, human intervention remained necessary for 24-30% of session time to provide emotional support. These findings empirically characterize human-AI collaboration boundaries in clinical rehabilitation, revealing both automation's potential to enhance efficiency and bridge digital divides, and the persistent necessity of human therapeutic presence—providing evidence for responsible deployment of AI-assisted healthcare systems.

contents.title: Making Absence Visible: The Roles of Reference and Prompting in Recognizing Missing Information
contents.abstract: Interactive systems that explain data, or support decision making often emphasize what is present while overlooking what is expected but missing. This presence bias limits users’ ability to form complete mental models of a dataset or situation. Detecting absence depends on expectations about what should be there, yet interfaces rarely help users form such expectations. We present an experimental study examining how reference framing and prompting influence people’s ability to recognize expected but missing categories in datasets. Participants compared distributions across three domains (energy, wealth, and regime) under two reference conditions: Global, presenting a unified population baseline, and Partial, showing several concrete exemplars. Results indicate that absence detection was higher with Partial reference than with Global reference, suggesting that partial, samples-based framing can support expectation formation and absence detection. When participants were prompted to look for what was missing, absence detection rose sharply. We discuss implications for interactive user interfaces and expectation-based visualization design, while considering cognitive trade-offs of reference structures and guided attention.

contents.title: NaviEdu: Dual-Path Knowledge Tracing for Detecting Knowledge Shifts and Driving Targeted Interventions
contents.abstract: AI-driven education systems seek to infer latent knowledge states and deliver adaptive feedback, enabling personalized learning at scale. Existing approaches span generation- and interaction-based methods as well as feedback-focused systems, but they are largely designed as loosely coupled pipelines. As a result, these methods often lack fine-grained modeling of evolving knowledge states and fail to detect sudden shifts, making it difficult to translate signals into effective personalized interventions.   To overcome these issues, we propose NaviEdu, a dual-path causal inference framework where a neural module captures non-linear dynamics and a graph module encodes structured reasoning. he two pathways are coordinated via consistency alignment, Laplacian regularization, and gradient-sensitive updates to keep coherent knowledge representations. A dynamic knowledge graph with feedback further enables a diagnose-intervene-re-diagnose cycle for personalized adaptation. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that NaviEdu consistently achieves state-of-the-art AUC and accuracy, with up to 2.2% improvements over strong baselines, while maintaining competitive RMSE and r^2.

contents.title: GuideAI: A Real-time Personalized Learning Solution with Adaptive Interventions
contents.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful learning tools, but they lack awareness of learners' cognitive and physiological states, limiting their adaptability to the user's learning style. Contemporary learning techniques primarily focus on structured learning paths, knowledge tracing, and generic adaptive testing but fail to address real-time learning challenges driven by cognitive load, attention fluctuations, and engagement levels. Building on findings from a formative user study (N=66), we introduce GuideAI, a multi-modal framework that enhances LLM-driven learning by integrating real-time biosensory feedback including eye gaze tracking, heart rate variability, posture detection, and digital note-taking behavior. GuideAI dynamically adapts learning content and pacing through cognitive optimizations (adjusting complexity based on learning progress markers), physiological interventions (breathing guidance and posture correction), and attention-aware strategies (redirecting focus using gaze analysis). Additionally,  GuideAI supports diverse learning modalities, including text-based, image-based, audio-based, and video-based instruction, across varied knowledge domains. A preliminary study (N = 25) assessed GuideAI’s impact on knowledge retention and cognitive load through standardized assessments. The results show statistically significant improvements in both problem-solving capability and recall-based knowledge assessments. Participants also experienced notable reductions in key NASA-TLX measures including mental demand, frustration levels, and effort, while simultaneously reporting enhanced perceived performance. These findings demonstrate GuideAI's potential to bridge the gap between current LLM-based learning systems and individualized learner needs, paving the way for adaptive, cognition-aware education at scale.

contents.title: Vulnerability of LLM Outputs to Heuristics-Inducing Prompt Structures
contents.abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have become indispensable tools in daily life. Although LLM applications have rapidly expanded across various domains, distorted outputs (specifically, bias and hallucination) are unresolved problems that threaten the reliability of LLM-based artificial intelligence (AI) agents. Focusing on internal mechanisms and social biases, prior research has rarely considered the possibility of distortion-induction even from non-malicious, ordinary prompts with specific input patterns.
This study empirically investigates whether LLMs exposed to certain prompt structures can induce biases commonly elicited in humans, namely, representativeness heuristics, anchoring heuristics, and framing heuristics. To this end, we constructed a test set that triggers one of the three heuristics and evaluated the outputs of state-of-the-art LLMs. We further examined the effectiveness of prompt engineering and debiasing interventions. The LLMs continued to produce heuristic-derived biased outputs under certain prompt conditions. Anchoring heuristics were observed at rates significantly above chance, whereas the representativeness and framing heuristics depended on the model and prompt structure. Debiasing interventions notably reduced the representativeness heuristics but exerted limited impact on anchoring and framing heuristics. 
This study highlights the need for enhanced awareness of vulnerabilities in LLM outputs against particular prompts. It also reveals that typical prompt-engineering strategies offer insufficient protection against such prompt structures. These results will contribute to the safe and effective use of LLMs in human–computer interactions and AI deployment.

contents.title: Mapping the Design Space of User Experience for Computer Use Agents
contents.abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based computer use agents execute user commands by interacting with available UI elements, but little is known about how users want to interact with these agents or what design factors matter for their user experience (UX). We conducted a two-phase study to map the UX design space for computer use agents. In Phase 1, we reviewed existing systems to develop a taxonomy of UX considerations, then refined it through interviews with eight UX and AI practitioners. The resulting taxonomy included categories such as user prompts, explainability, user control, and users’ mental models, with corresponding subcategories and example design features. In Phase 2, we ran a Wizard-of-Oz study with 20 participants, where a researcher acted as a web-based computer use agent and probed user reactions during normal, error-prone and risky execution. We used the findings to validate the taxonomy from Phase 1 and deepen our understand of the design space by identifying the connections between design areas and divergence in user needs and scenarios. Our taxonomy and empirical insights provide a map for developers to consider different aspects of user experience in computer use agent design and to situate their designs within users' diverse needs and scenarios.

contents.title: SCSimulator: An Exploratory Visual Analytics Framework for Partner Selection in Supply Chains through LLM-driven Multi-Agent Simulation
contents.abstract: Supply chains (SCs), complex networks spanning from raw material acquisition to product delivery, with enterprises as interconnected nodes, play a pivotal role in organizational success. However, optimizing SCs remains challenging, particularly in partner selection, a key bottleneck shaped by both competitive and cooperative dynamics. This challenge inherently constitutes a multi-objective dynamic game requiring a synergistic integration of Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) and Game Theory (GT). Traditional approaches, grounded in mathematical simplifications and managerial heuristics, often fail to capture real-world intricacies and risk introducing subjective biases. Multi-agent simulation (MAS) offers promise, but prior research has largely relied on fixed, uniform agent logic, limiting practical applicability. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) create new opportunities to represent complex SC requirements and hybrid game logic. However, challenges persist in modeling dynamic SC relationships, ensuring interpretability, and balancing agent autonomy with expert control. To address these issues, we present SCSimulator, an exploratory visual analytics framework that integrates LLM-driven MAS with human-in-the-loop collaboration for SC partner selection. SCSimulator simulates SC evolution via adaptive network structures and enterprise behaviors, which are visualized via interpretable interfaces. By combining Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning with explainable AI (XAI) techniques, the framework generates multi-faceted, transparent explanations of decision trade-offs. Users can iteratively adjust simulation settings to explore outcomes aligned with their expectations and strategic priorities. Developed through iterative co-design with SC experts and industry managers, SCSimulator serves as a proof-of-concept, offering both methodological contributions and practical insights for future research on SC decision-making and interactive AI-driven analytics. Usage scenarios and a user study further demonstrate the system's effectiveness and usability.

contents.title: A Multimodal Investigation of Controllability and Cognitive Load in Interactive Machine Learning
contents.abstract:
Interactive Machine Learning (IML) systems promise to democratize AI by enabling human influence over model behavior, yet the cognitive and behavioral implications of user control remain understudied. We present an investigation of how system controllability affects human factors in IML through the lens of an extensible research platform designed for both pedagogical and research applications. Our system features a toggleable hyperparameter control panel that transforms a streamlined annotation interface into an adjustable learning environment, allowing users to directly manipulate model training dynamics including learning rates, optimizers, and regularization parameters. Through a controlled laboratory study with 46 participants performing Named Entity Recognition (NER) tasks, we used multimodal measurements combining subjective assessments (NASA-TLX), physiological measures (pupil dilation, galvanic skin response), and behavioral metrics to understand the human cost of algorithmic control. 

Our findings reveal a fundamental tension in IML design: while controllability is often theorized to support agency, it significantly increases cognitive load and task completion time, with users requiring significantly more time when given control options.

These findings have implications for the design of human-AI collaborative systems. Our work contributes: (1) a research platform for studying human factors in IML for NLP that includes controllability manipulation and real-time online updates, (2) empirical evidence of the cognitive costs of algorithmic control, and (3) design implications for cognitive sustainability in IML systems. As AI systems become increasingly integrated into professional workflows and educational contexts, understanding these human factors is crucial for creating IML systems that are not only powerful but also cognitively sustainable for long-term use.

contents.title: Improving Human Verification of LLM Reasoning through Interactive Explanation Interfaces
contents.abstract: The reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) have led to their increasing employment in several critical applications, particularly education, where they support problem-solving, tutoring, and personalized study. While there are a plethora of works showing the effectiveness of LLMs in generating step-by-step solutions through chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning on reasoning benchmarks, little is understood about whether the generated CoT is helpful for end-users in improving their ability to comprehend mathematical reasoning problems and detect errors/hallucinations in LLM-generated solutions. To address this gap and contribute to understanding how reasoning can improve human-AI interaction, we present three new interactive reasoning interfaces: interactive CoT (iCoT), interactive Program-of-Thought (iPoT), and interactive Graph (iGraph), and a novel framework that generates the LLM's reasoning from traditional CoT to alternative, interactive formats. Across 125 participants, we found that interactive interfaces significantly improved performance. Specifically, the iGraph interface yielded the highest clarity and error detection rate (85.6 %), followed by iPoT (82.5 %), iCoT (80.6 %), all outperforming standard CoT (73.5 %). Interactive interfaces also led to faster response times, where participants using iGraph were fastest (57.9 secs), compared to iCoT and iPoT (60 secs), and the standard CoT baseline (64.7 secs). Furthermore, participants preferred the iGraph reasoning interface, citing its superior ability to enable users to follow the LLM's reasoning process. We discuss the implications of these results and provide recommendations for the future design of reasoning models. The code and interfaces for this project can be found here: https://github.com/Runtaozhou/Interactive-CoT.

contents.title: "Same Voice, Different Language": An Exploration of Voice-Cloned Translation to Support Non-Native Speakers in Online Meetings
contents.abstract: Cross-lingual meetings have become essential for global collaboration, yet current translation technologies often strip away vocal identity — the unique speaker characteristics that convey nuance and social presence. While generic text-to-speech (TTS) provides basic intelligibility, it creates a disconnect between speakers and their translated voices, potentially undermining engagement and comprehension. This paper investigates whether voice cloning technology can bridge this gap by preserving speaker identity in real-time translation. We present a controlled study comparing four voice conditions in meeting interpretation: original speech, gender-neutral TTS, gender-matched TTS, and voice cloning. Through a within-subjects experiment with 45 participants, we demonstrate that voice cloning significantly reduces mental workload ($p < .001$) and enhances user experience across pragmatic quality ($p < .001$), hedonic quality ($p < .001$), and overall satisfaction ($p < .001$) compared to traditional TTS. While original speech maintained advantages in naturalness, voice cloning achieved superior intelligibility, social impression, and user preference. Qualitative analysis revealed that participants valued voice cloning for preserving speaker identity and improving conversation tracking in multi-speaker scenarios. Our findings suggest that identity-preserving translation represents a significant advancement for cross-lingual communication systems, offering both cognitive and social benefits. We conclude with design implications for integrating voice cloning into meeting platforms while addressing ethical considerations around consent and transparency.

contents.title: Balancing Flow and Collaboration: Exploring Visual Noise Cancellation in Mixed Reality Workspace
contents.abstract: In open-plan offices, visual noise from surrounding people and objects can negatively impact both concentration and mood. Mixed Reality (MR) offers a promising approach to address this challenge by reshaping the workspace. In this paper, we first conducted a survey with 50 office workers to examine the impact of visual noise, identifying common sources of distraction and potential mitigation strategies. Considering the necessity of face-to-face communication in office environments, we designed adaptive user interfaces to strike a balance between deep focus and seamless in-situ collaboration. We utilized Virtual Reality (VR) and Diminished Reality (DR) methods to eliminate visual noise and leveraged face orientation along with a distance threshold to determine collaborative intentions. We developed a prototype system and conducted a user study for evaluation. The results indicate that our system can create a tranquil workspace to foster concentration and workplace well-being, while maintaining necessary in-situ collaboration. These findings provide valuable insights for designing future MR-integrated office environments.

contents.title: PersonaMail: Learning and Adapting Personal Communication Preferences for Context-Aware Email Writing
contents.abstract: LLM-assisted writing has seen rapid adoption in interpersonal communication, yet current systems often fail to capture the subtle tones essential for effectiveness. Email writing exemplifies this challenge: effective messages require careful alignment with intent, relationship, and context beyond mere fluency. Through formative studies, we identified three key challenges: articulating nuanced communicative intent, making modifications at multiple levels of granularity, and reusing effective tone strategies across messages. We developed PersonaMail, a system that addresses these gaps through structured communication factor exploration, granular editing controls, and adaptive reuse of successful strategies. Our evaluation compared PersonaMail against standard LLM interfaces, and showed improved efficiency in both immediate and repeated use, alongside higher user satisfaction. We contribute design implications for AI-assisted communication systems that prioritize interpersonal nuance over generic text generation.

contents.title: Understanding Reader Perception Shifts upon Disclosure of AI Authorship
contents.abstract: As AI writing support becomes ubiquitous, the question of how disclosing its use affects reader perception remains critical and underexplored. We conducted a controlled study with 261 participants to examine how disclosing varying levels of AI involvement shifts perceptions of the author across six distinct communicative acts. Our analysis of 990 evaluations reveals that disclosure generally erodes perceived trustworthiness, caring, competence, and likability, with the most precipitous declines observed in social and interpersonal writing. A thematic analysis of participant feedback attributes these negative shifts to a perceived loss of human sincerity, diminished authorial effort, and the contextual inappropriateness of AI. Notably, however, we find that higher AI literacy mitigates these negative perceptions, leading to greater tolerance or even appreciation for AI assistance. Our results highlight the nuanced social dynamics of AI-mediated authorship and inform design implications for transparent, context-sensitive writing systems that better preserve trust and authenticity.

contents.title: DesignBridge: Bridging Designer Expertise and User Preferences through AI-Enhanced Co-Design for Fashion
contents.abstract: Effective collaboration between designers and users is important for fashion design, which can increase the user acceptance of fashion products and thereby create value. However, it remains an enduring challenge, as traditional designer-centric approaches restrict meaningful user participation, while user-driven methods demand design proficiency, often marginalizing professional creative judgment. Current co-design practices, including workshops and AI-assisted frameworks, struggle with low user engagement, inefficient preference collection, and difficulties in balancing user feedback with design considerations. To address these challenges, we conducted a formative study with designers and users experienced in co-design (N=7), identifying critical challenges for current collaboration between designers and users in the co-design process, and their requirements. Informed by these insights, we introduce DesignBridge, a multi-platform AI-enhanced interactive system that bridges designer expertise and user preferences through three stages: (1) Initial Design Framing, where designers define initial concepts. (2) Preference Expression Collection, where users intuitively articulate preferences via interactive tools. (3) Preference-Integrated Design, where designers use AI-assisted analytics to integrate feedback into cohesive designs. A user study demonstrates that DesignBridge significantly enhances user preference collection and analysis, enabling designers to integrate diverse preferences with professional expertise.

contents.title: CritiQ AI and SixHats Engine: AI-as-Judge Systems for Early-Stage Evaluation and Preference Formation in Collaborative Ideation
contents.abstract: We present CritiQ AI and SixHats Engine, two web-based systems designed to extend AI-supported ideation beyond idea generation by providing structured evaluation and multi-perspective critique. Rather than producing new ideas, the tools help student teams examine the strengths, weaknesses, assumptions, and trade-offs of their proposals, which supports the early stages of idea selection and refinement. Combined with instructor oversight, these systems scaffold the evaluative and rationale-building work that precedes and informs convergence decisions in project-based courses.

contents.title: Eterna: An AI-Powered Journaling System for Contextual Memory Preservation and Digital Legacy Creation
contents.abstract: Eterna is a mobile journaling and memory-preservation system designed to help individuals capture meaningful life moments with contextual richness rather than the performance-oriented habits shaped by social media. The system integrates multimodal AI to guide users through reflective narration, overcoming the “blank page” problem common in journaling. Eterna supports both everyday memory capture and long-term autobiographical work, offering a private, secure space for building a personal memory capsule or digital legacy. In this demo, we present Eterna Journal, which enables users to upload images, receive context-building prompts, narrate memories through text or speech, and generate structured journal entries. We describe the system architecture, use cases, ethical design considerations, and the technical implementation based on React Native, Supabase, and Google’s Gemini API. The demo allows attendees to experience Eterna’s memory-preservation workflow and glimpse future capabilities of Eterna Legacy.

contents.title: fCrit: Towards an AI-Enabled Reflection-In-Action Interface for Furniture Design
contents.abstract: Design insights are elusive. Capturing and transforming them requires a deliberate command of language through iterative sensemaking and reflection. Yet current GenAI use bypasses this intricate work, prioritizing automated content generation over supporting genuine creative sensemaking. We present fCrit, a conversational AI system that scaffolds formal analysis through adaptive levels of reflexivity. Through a preliminary pilot study with 13 designers, we observe three scaffolding features that may support aesthetic sensemaking while preserving creative agency: convergent reflection, divergent exploration, and mixed-initiative engagement. This formative study also surfaces an interface challenge: insights that emerge through dialogues are difficult to document and transform into interactive, tangible artifacts for users. To address this, we propose two interface features for future validation—manipulable insight environments and Feature Grab. We argue that AI systems for design learning should scaffold comprehension through interactive features before content production.

contents.title: CausalAgent: A Conversational Multi-Agent System for End-to-End Causal Inference
contents.abstract: Causal inference holds immense value in fields such as healthcare, economics, and social sciences. However, traditional causal analysis workflows impose significant technical barriers, requiring researchers to possess dual backgrounds in statistics and computer science, while manually selecting algorithms, handling data quality issues, and interpreting complex results. To address these challenges, we propose CausalAgent, a conversational multi-agent system for end-to-end causal inference. The system innovatively integrates Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to achieve automation from data cleaning and causal structure learning to bias correction and report generation through natural language interaction. Users need only upload a dataset and pose questions in natural language to receive a rigorous, interactive analysis report. As a novel user-centered human-AI collaboration paradigm, CausalAgent explicitly models the analysis workflow. By leveraging interactive visualizations, it significantly lowers the barrier to entry for causal analysis while ensuring the rigor and interpretability of the process.

contents.title: ExPeerience: Support Student–AI Co-Creation in a Learnersourcing Workflow
contents.abstract: This demo presents ExPeerience, an AI-assisted learnersourcing system that supports students in co-creating contextualized worked-out examples for database programming. Learnersourcing is a pedagogical approach in which students act as contributors by creating learning materials as part of the learning process. The system addresses two challenges in learnersourcing: (1) the high cognitive burden students face during content creation, and (2) the difficulty of balancing student learning with the production of reusable, high-quality sourced learning materials. ExPeerience structures student–AI collaboration by assigning the AI distinct roles across ideation, content generation, and solution evaluation, while keeping students responsible for decision-making and problem-solving.

affiliations.dsl: School of Applied and Creative Computing

contents.title: Demonstrating Domain-informed Summarization through Contrast (DiSCo)
contents.abstract: Intelligent interfaces increasingly rely on large language models (LLMs) to summarize user-generated content. While effective at condensing what is explicitly mentioned, known as presence bias, such summaries systematically overlook what is expected yet missing. This presence bias can mislead users by obscuring diagnostic gaps that matter for decision making. We present a demonstration of \textit{DiSCo} (Domain-informed Summarization through Contrast), a method for review summarization that explicitly surfaces both salient and absent information. The system constructs domain-level topical expectations from comparable entities and contrasts them with individual review profiles to identify over-represented and missing aspects. These contrasts are then integrated into summaries using LLMs, allowing users to explore what is present and stands out and what is missing compared to other entities in the domain. The demo showcases how DiSCo summaries change user interaction with review content across multiple accommodation domains. Participants can interactively toggle expectation views, inspect missing aspects, and compare standard LLM summaries with DiSCo summaries in real time. The demonstration illustrates how expectation-aware interfaces support transparency, reflection, and better-informed decision making.

contents.title: Neural Notes: An Online Platform for Real-Time Structured Musical Improvisation Supporting Human–Human, Human–AI, and AI–AI Interaction
contents.abstract: Neural Notes is an online platform for real-time structured musical improvisation supporting human–human, human–AI, and AI–AI interaction. The system supports real-time improvisatory exchange with low perceived latency, situated within a structured harmonic environment defined by user-selected songs. The platform runs in any modern web browser and supports both MIDI-enabled instruments and computer keyboard input, making it widely accessible to musicians of all levels. Neural Notes provides a unified environment for co-creative musical interaction, offering researchers a living testbed for studying improvisational dynamics, AI-assisted practice, and multi-agent musical collaboration. In this demo, we showcase all three interaction modes and highlight how the platform enables responsive musical dialogue between humans and AI agents, serving as both a creative tool and a foundation for future research in algorithmic music composition and intelligent interactive music systems.

contents.title: EvalAgent: Interactive Comparative Evaluation of Computer-Using GUI Agents
contents.abstract: As generalist Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) gain popularity, evaluating them solely based on task completion is insufficient to understand their reasoning processes, assess their robustness, or ensure that their actions align with user intentions. Developers and end-users lack systematic tools to compare the behaviors and reasoning processes of different agents or configurations, and to assess their alignment with user preferences and values. To address this gap, we present EvalAgent, an interactive system designed for criteria-based evaluation of LLM-powered web agents using an LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm. EvalAgent enables users to (1) configure agent models and personas as experimental conditions; (2) visualize execution trajectories as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs); and (3) employ Context-Aware Judge---a multi-scale auditing mechanism that dynamically adjusts the granularity of LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations based on user-defined criteria. Beyond binary pass/fail verdicts, EvalAgent highlights relevant segments within execution traces to surface how and why decisions were made. This workflow supports fine-grained, full-trace behavioral auditing, helping bridge the gap between high-level policy constraints, nuanced user preferences and values, and low-level agent execution.

contents.title: Speech-Driven Immersive Analytics: GenAI-grounded Sensemaking Perspective
contents.abstract: Research on natural interactions between humans and immersive analytics systems has increasingly focused on the role of natural language interactions (NLIs), especially speech, as generative AI (GenAI) has become popular. Speech is a natural modality that enables users to express intent with a low learning curve and allows analytic systems to infer that intent with richer contextual information. However, it remains underexplored across various fields. Existing work still has gaps in understanding natural interactions and focuses more on multimodal interactions with speech as an auxiliary modality or textual modalities. This dissertation explores this challenge through a systematic approach: (1) the examination of the interplay between users’ speech patterns, embodiment tendencies within speech, as well as observed embodied cues in conveying analytic intent; and (2) the design of methods for speech-oriented interactions between humans and intelligent analytic systems in immersive environments, specifically from a data-sensemaking perspective.

contents.title: Modelling Prompting Behaviours in LLM-Mediated Task Solving
contents.abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become routine study partners, assignment outputs increasingly obscure what students actually learn. This PhD investigates how learning-related signals become visible in student–LLM interaction, and how temporal and semantic prompting behaviours relate to short-term learning gains in programming tasks. The work models student behaviour during LLM-mediated problem-solving and examines how these models can support adaptive feedback and reflection. Rather than treating the LLM as a neutral tool, the assistant is considered part of an intelligent interface whose responses shape student confidence, verification, and revision behaviour. These behaviour–outcome relationships are used to develop automated analyses that translate detected patterns into brief, targeted prompting recommendations. The project aims to evaluate these models and recommendations through progressively larger studies, contributing evidence on how user modelling can support more reflective and effective use of LLMs in education.

contents.title: Human-AI Collaboration for Goal Achievement in Physical Activity Tracking
contents.abstract: Physical activity (PA) is fundamental to long-term health, yet many people struggle to sustain engagement with existing PA tracking systems. Despite their widespread adoption, current trackers offer limited support for personalised, adaptive guidance throughout users’ physical activity journeys which leads to disengagement, disrupted behavioural routines, and reduced potential for long- term behaviour change. While prior work has incorporated AI to generate personalised predictions and recommendations, such sys- tems typically treat both objective and self-reported data as inputs for modelling rather than as resources for ongoing negotiation, and focus on isolated functions such as goal prediction instead of supporting users across the entire PA journey. This PhD aims to re- define how AI can contribute to behaviour change by investigating human–AI collaboration across the entire PA cycle, including goal setting, planning, tracking, and reflection. Instead of positioning AI as a prescriptive analytics engine, this work explores how AI can act as a collaborative partner that adapts to users’ behavioural con- text, fosters user’s agency, self- efficacy, and strengthens long-term engagement. The research will generate new interaction mecha- nisms, transparency models, and human-AI collaborative PA track- ing strategies. Ultimately, this work seeks to advance foundational understanding of effective human–AI collaboration for behaviour change and provide design knowledge for the next generation of personalised, trustworthy PA support systems.

contents.title: Human-AI Schema Discovery and Application for Creative Problem Solving
contents.abstract: Humans often rely on underlying structural patterns—schemas—to create, whether by writing stories, designing software, or composing music. Schemas help organize ideas and guide exploration, but they are often difficult to discover and apply, especially in complex or unfamiliar domains. My Ph.D. research develops a framework for human-AI schema discovery and application to support creative problem solving. I design systems that support users in sensemaking over examples to abstract schemas, and in operationalizing schemas into human-AI co-creative workflows for application. This research offers insights into how schema-guided interaction can make implicit knowledge more accessible and actionable, advancing more transparent and collaborative human-AI systems.

contents.title: Personal Reflection through Adaptive Data Comics: A Story on Reading Data
contents.abstract: Data comics are an appealing medium for visualizing personal taste and interests. As a design study, this paper presents an interactive data comic that analyzes personal reading data. It adapts both content and structure to users' data and selections. Combining elements of data visualization, sequential storytelling, and user interactivity, the comic transforms the data into a relatable narrative organized in different chapters. Accompanying the visualizations, data analysis guidance and book recommendations are generated specific to the personal data through a large language model. An exploratory user study with six participants investigated the impact of the comic on user experience.

contents.title: Towards Adaptive Mixed-Initiative Media Recommender Systems with LLMs
contents.abstract: Recommender systems (RS) increasingly shape how people encounter digital media, but often leave users with limited understanding of why items are recommended and few ways to influence them. Existing user interfaces (UIs) typically offer static, one-size-fits-all explanations and controls that do not reflect users' dynamic needs, limiting user agency. My research investigates how mixed-initiative recommender interfaces, enabled by large language models (LLMs), can adapt explanatory and interactive elements to users' dynamic needs. I plan to develop a conceptual model that links situational media needs to requirements for information (understanding) and interaction (control). Building on this model, I will design and experimentally evaluate adaptive UIs that combine personalized explanations with interactive controls, measuring their effects on user understanding and perceived control. Through this doctoral consortium, I seek feedback on my research plan and guidance in designing effective experiments on adaptive mixed-initiative RS.

contents.title: From Data to Dialogue: A Multi-Agent Chatbot Interface for Decision Support in Guide Dog Training
contents.abstract: Guide dog training programs collect extensive behavioral, medical, and performance information for every dog, including the Behavioral Checklist (BCL), which can contain over 50 detailed assessment items per dog. Trainers rely on these records, together with data from thousands of past dogs, to judge whether a dog is on track, where it is struggling, and whether it is likely to succeed in the training program. Furthermore, although all this information is stored in organizational digital databases, trainers often can not use it in practice because the data requires technical processing, aggregation, and interpretation. To bridge these gaps, we present a multi-agent conversational chatbot that embeds the predictive model and the data processing pipeline inside a dialogue-based interface. Instead of navigating complex databases or analytical tools, trainers can ask natural-language questions such as “How does this dog compare to past dogs with similar profiles?” Our suggested chatbot retrieves relevant information, analyzes it using structured data and model outputs, and presents clear information that supports daily training decisions. We conducted a formative study with three professional experienced guide dog trainers across three main tasks: information analysis, dog comparison, and success prediction. Trainers reported that the chatbot made the underlying information more approachable, more interpretable, and easier to apply when making decisions about each dog’s training trajectory.

contents.title: Beyond the Conversational Paradigm: Models, Design Principles, and Tools for Advancing the Spectrum of Human-LLM Interaction
contents.abstract: Interaction with Large Language Models (LLMs) is expanding beyond strictly Conversational User Interfaces (CUIs), with systems starting to incorporate direct-manipulation mechanisms, typical of Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs), into their designs. We argue that the current landscape of human–LLM interfaces can be described as a continuum, ranging from strictly conversational to increasingly visual and interactive interfaces built on top of a basic CUI. Based on this assumption, the doctoral research presented in this paper aims to model and analyze this CUI–GUI spectrum. Grounded in a Human-Centered Design methodology and a research-through-design approach, the work investigates factors influencing users’ preferences for different interface configurations along the spectrum, through comparative empirical studies, and leveraging representative interface prototypes across it. Insights from these studies will inform the design of adaptive LLM interfaces that dynamically adjust the balance between conversational and graphical elements in response to users’ needs and contextual factors. In parallel, the research will explore both risks and benefits associated with different regions of the spectrum, with the ultimate goal of developing a comprehensive characterization, as well as a set of design principles and tools that can effectively guide the design of the next generation of human–LLM interfaces.

contents.title: Enhancing Vignette-Based Research Through AI-Generated Storyboards
contents.abstract:
The representational form of vignettes is often treated as incidental in scenario-based research, despite its potential impact on participants’ understanding and reasoning processes of complex situations. This work outlines the research pipeline to transform
text-based vignettes into low-fidelity storyboards using generative AI that remain faithful to the original text, visually consistent across conditions, and neutral with respect to unintended interpretive cues. Rather than aiming for visual realism, the pipeline
prioritizes narrative clarity, stylistic simplicity, and experimental control, allowing representational format to be examined as a methodological factor. By making storyboard generation reproducible and transparent, this work highlights presentation modality as
an important consideration in vignette-based research and provides a foundation for studying how visual storyboards may affect cognitive load, interpretation of experimental manipulations, and perceived communicative quality in intelligent user interface and
human-AI interaction research.

contents.title: Feasibility of Activity Fingerprints for Glucose Prediction
contents.abstract: Proactive health interfaces require accurate forecasting of physiological states to deliver timely interventions. In this work-in-progress, we investigate the feasibility of predicting short-term glucose dynamics in older adults with Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) using "activity fingerprints" - frequency-domain features derived from wearable posture signals. Analyzing data from 105 participants, we utilize Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to capture individualized movement rhythms. Granger causality analysis identifies these rhythms as leading indicators of glucose changes with a predictive horizon of approximately 40 minutes. We demonstrate that an XGBoost model utilizing these fingerprints outperforms standard baselines (Mean Prediction and Last-Value-Carried-Forward). These preliminary findings validate the potential of posture dynamics as a non-invasive predictor, laying the technical groundwork for future intelligent interfaces capable of prompting timely glucose measurements.

contents.title: Understanding and Designing AI Systems that Reshape Social Dynamics in Group Decision-Making
contents.abstract:
As AI integrates into teams and organizations, it can influence how people communicate, collaborate, and make decisions. I study AI not only as a tool, but as a social actor that can reconfigure group dynamics, power relations, and collective reasoning. My dissertation asks how AI changes group thinking, decision-making, and value exploration, and how we can design it to support more inclusive outcomes. So far, I have completed two studies. 
First, I conducted a controlled experiment with three LLM agents to investigate the influence patterns of majority, minority, and diffusion over time, and I observed distinct trajectories of opinion change and confidence across tasks. 
Second, I developed an LLM-powered system that supports minority voices in power-imbalanced group discussions by providing counterarguments or AI-mediated messages. The results show a clear trade-off between participation and psychological safety. 
Next, I will develop a Value Constellation dashboard that visualizes hidden value conflicts to prompt group reflection and reduce groupthink.

contents.title: SmartUI: Human-in-the-Loop Editable Interface Generation through Semantic Structures
contents.abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming interface design by enabling automatic layout generation from natural language prompts. However, existing generative UI systems primarily focus on one-shot synthesis and lack structural editability, limiting designers’ ability to iteratively refine and reuse generated results. This paper presents SmartUI, a Figma-based, human-in-the-loop generative interface design plugin that supports fine-grained user editing throughout the entire design workflow, including both pre-generation requirement refinement and post-generation interface modification. SmartUI introduces a Structured UI Language (SUL) to represent design intent, hierarchical logic, and component relationships, establishing a high-fidelity mapping between semantic input and interface structure. Based on a statistical analysis of 7,381 webpages, we develop a high-frequency taxonomy of container and non-container components, providing a stable semantic foundation for layout generation. Experimental results demonstrate that SmartUI achieves a structure consistency of 0.93 and significantly improves generation speed, component reuse rate, and reduces modification operations compared to manual workflows. These findings highlight SmartUI’s potential to enable editable, efficient, and reusable generative design in human-centered creation processes. We will release the smartUI to https://github.com/sssala/SmartUI

contents.title: A Privacy-Preserving Hypercube Framework for Interactive Recommendations
contents.abstract: Interactive decision-support systems increasingly rely on cloud-based recommender architectures, which introduce latency, energy consumption, and privacy concerns that may negatively affect user experience and trust. In this paper, we present a content-based, on-device recommendation framework in which all inference is performed locally, without server-side computation or data sharing. The approach is based on a lightweight hypercube representation of items, enabling efficient and interpretable personalization. The system is designed to operate across multiple domains, including movies and restaurants, and supports extensions that incorporate side information, such as features extracted from textual reviews. We report initial qualitative findings from a user study in the movie domain, focusing on users’ perceptions of usability, personalization, and privacy.

contents.title: Multi-Agent Framework for Adaptive Social-Emotional Learning in Children's Digital Communication
contents.abstract: In today’s digital society, children are increasingly exposed to online communication environments at an early age, often before they have developed the social and emotional skills required to manage misunderstandings, conflicts, and emotionally charged interactions. We present Lilo, an interactive multi-agent conversational system designed to help children aged 8 to 11 practice safe, empathetic, and reflective digital communication. Lilo offers scenario-based chat simulations that resemble familiar messaging platforms, allowing children to experiment with communication strategies in a protected environment without real-world social consequences. The system integrates adaptive conversational agents with a structured intervention mechanism based on the Stop–Think–Act pedagogical framework. This demo paper presents the system design, interaction model, and educational rationale underlying Lilo, and demonstrates how large language models can be leveraged to support social and emotional learning through guided digital interaction.

contents.title: filescape: A Non-Hierarchical, Privacy-Preserving Visual AI System for Personal File Organization and Exploration
contents.abstract:
We present filescape, a non-hierarchical, privacy-preserving visual AI system for personal file organization and exploration. As inexpensive, persistent digital storage has expanded personal file collections across devices, folder hierarchies and keyword search have become increasingly brittle for re-finding and sense-making.

filescape presents personal documents as a 2D embedding-based spatial map built with lightweight, locally hosted embedding and language models, providing an at-a-glance overview for rapid re-finding, pattern recognition, and open-ended exploration. filescape integrates with existing operating-system workflows (e.g., macOS Finder) and reflects filesystem changes in real time. The interface combines WYSIWYG previews, interactive clustering, and semantic navigation to support exploration without sacrificing privacy or requiring cloud services. A pilot study comparing filescape to macOS Finder suggests that embedding-based spatial interfaces can improve re-finding efficiency and user experience.

contents.title: Chatbots or Apps? Design Tradeoffs and the Need for Hybrid Health Interfaces
contents.abstract: Large language model (LLM)–based chatbots are emerging as persuasive interfaces in digital health, yet their advantages relative to established mobile apps remain uncertain. We present a comparative field study (N = 167) evaluating two smoking-cessation digital interventions: QuitBot, an LLM-driven conversational coach, and quitSTART, a structured mobile app. Participants selected one of these, used it for a week, and reported on usability, engagement, and perceived effectiveness. Quantitative findings showed that QuitBot was experienced as easier and more natural to use, while quitSTART prompted more frequent daily engagement and a greater proportion of users to plan a quit date. Qualitative analysis revealed parallel strengths: QuitBot fostered empathy, motivation, and social presence, whereas quitSTART reinforced predictability, goal-setting, and progress tracking. Together, the findings highlight a potential trade-off between relational adaptivity and structural scaffolding in persuasive health interfaces, suggesting that hybrid intelligent systems integrating both dimensions may better sustain engagement and support long-term behavior change.

contents.title: From Verification Burden to Trusted Collaboration: Design Goals for LLM-Assisted Literature Reviews
contents.abstract: Although numerous studies have examined how researchers use Large Language Models (LLMs) in academic writing, their concrete implementation, limitations, and design challenges in the literature review process remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a user study with researchers across multiple disciplines that characterize current practices, benefits, and pain points when using LLMs to investigate related work. We identified three recurring gaps: (i) lack of trust in outputs, (ii) a persistent verification burden, and (iii) fragmentation across tools. To address these gaps, we propose six design goals and a high-level framework that operationalizes them through improved visualization of related papers, verification at every step, and human-feedback alignment with generation-guided explanations.

contents.title: RAMA: Regulation Aware Multimodal Alignment for Misbranding Detection in Product Advertisements
contents.abstract: Misbranding of health-related products poses significant risks to public safety and regulatory compliance. Existing approaches to claim verification largely rely on keyword matching or generic text classification, failing to capture the nuanced reasoning required to align product claims with legal statutes. In this work, we introduce RAMA: A Regulation Aware Multimodal Alignment for Misbranding Detection, a novel framework that formulates misbranding detection between product claims and regulatory provisions. Leveraging a curated dataset of FDA warning letters, we construct structured representations of claims and statutes using natural logic trees, enabling interpretable and rule-consistent reasoning. Our model integrates a regulation-aware gating mechanism with a contrastive alignment objective to jointly optimize misbranding classification and statute mapping. Extensive experiments on the \textsc{FDA-Misbrand} dataset demonstrate that RAMA significantly outperforms strong baselines across accuracy, F1-score, and regulation alignment metrics, while providing interpretable attention patterns that highlight critical linguistic cues.

contents.title: AIoT Smart Home Automation Architecture: Autonomous Decision-Making Powered by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)
contents.abstract: Traditional smart home systems often rely on heterogeneous sensors and rigid rule-based pipelines, which limit scalability, flexibility, and ease of deployment. We propose a highly extensible smart home framework driven by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), utilizing a single camera as the minimal sensing unit for unified home environment perception, structured labeling, and autonomous decision-making. Our system automatically generates standardized tags, such as cleanliness, object locations, and human activities, to enable functions like cleaning scheduling, health reporting, and object tracking. These tags can also be extended to organize and integrate additional data sources, supporting comprehensive and scalable smart home management. Preliminary results demonstrate the feasibility and promise of this single-camera, MLLM-powered approach for smart home automation.

contents.title: SitWell: An Intelligent User Interface for Real-Time Posture Monitoring and Clinically-Informed Feedback
contents.abstract: Prolonged sitting is a significant health concern, linked to musculoskeletal disorders and chronic pain. While existing smart chairs often provide generic alerts, they lack the personalization necessary for long-term behavior change, especially for individuals with pre-existing conditions. We present SitWell, an intelligent User Interface (UI) that leverages dual pressure-mapping, cloud-based machine learning, and Large Language Models (LLMs) to deliver personalized, context-aware postural coaching. The system employs a user-tailored Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on individual pressure data, achieving above 90% accuracy in classifying custom postures. A novel contribution is the integration of LLM, which analyzes a user’s postural timeline and clinical profile to generate actionable, personalized recommendations. SitWell demonstrates how personalized AI and generative models can be integrated into a human-centered interface to bridge the gap between clinical expertise and daily health maintenance.

contents.title: Understanding EEG Microstate–Based Dementia Detection with Explainable AI
contents.abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a leading cause of dementia worldwide, and early detection plays a critical role in enabling timely care and improving quality of life. While deep learning models based on electroencephalography (EEG) microstate representations show strong potential for early AD detection, their complex temporal behaviour generally makes decisions difficult for humans to understand and rely on. This paper presents an explainable approach for temporal EEG microstate classifiers, which helps users understand how brain dynamics contribute to individual predictions. The proposed method produces time-aligned, microstate-aware explanations that reveal which temporal segments and microstate patterns influence AD and normal control decisions. Developed based on a fixed, pre-trained microstate-based dementia detection model across multiple EEG datasets, the approach improves the transparency and interpretability of AI decisions. By translating model behaviour into meaningful temporal brain patterns, this work advances a human-centred approach towards trustworthy AI for EEG-based clinical decision-making for more precise outcomes.

contents.title: Looking Back to the Future: Characterizing Feedback Loops in Adaptive Systems
contents.abstract: We examine adaptive systems wherein feedback loops adjust system properties to enhance the user experience or interaction performance. Through examples it is illustrated that the range of duration for feedback loops is huge, from less than one second to months, even years. To fully encapsulate the range of possibilities, a modified time-scale of human action model is presented. The model uses world views and bands to position human actions by their duration. Using a log scale, actions are positioned in bands with duration ranging from less than one second to years and beyond. This paper is a call to research in intelligent and adaptive systems to more fully describe, quantify, and characterize the feedback loops for adaptation.

contents.title: Designing Persuasive Messages Using Hypothesis Generation from Large Language Models
contents.abstract: Designing persuasive messages is central to intelligent user interfaces such as mobile health apps and chatbots, yet predicting which features of language drive persuasiveness remains a challenge. This paper presents a data-driven framework that integrates human judgments, large language models (LLMs), and machine learning to generate hypotheses about persuasive message design. In a study with N = 1,348 participants evaluating 174 messages to reduce drinking behavior, message-level preference varied widely (19–74%), motivating the search for systematic drivers of persuasion. Using ChatGPT as a hypothesis generator, the prompting surfaced plausible features such as framing outcomes as personal benefits, highlighting positive consequences, emphasizing concrete facts, orienting toward the future, and invoking protective health. This hybrid approach demonstrates how interpretable machine learning can extend beyond prediction to discovery, enabling researchers and designers to iteratively uncover the mechanisms of persuasion. This work contributes a generalizable method for hypothesis generation in persuasive systems, with implications for adaptive message design, behavior change interventions, and human-centered AI.

contents.title: Designing for Social Collaboration and Human-AI Alignment
contents.abstract: As digital communication increasingly shifts toward AI-mediated environments, maintaining emotional nuance and social alignment remains a critical challenge. While communication tools like emojis have traditionally bridged the gap left by absent face-to-face signals, the integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) introduces a new paradigm of interaction, changing how social cues are interpreted and expressed. The main objectives of this research are to address the gap between LLM understanding and human value-sensitivity through three key areas: (a) analyzing how existing tools like emojis shape emotional clarity, (b) designing a chatbot to facilitate social connection during initial interactions between strangers, and (c) developing a collaborative system for stakeholders to co-create evaluation criteria for "LLM-as-a-judge" workflows. By focusing on these interventions, my work seeks to ensure that the evolution of AI-mediated collaboration preserves the essential nuances of human values and intentional social alignment.

contents.title: Is Feedback All You Need? Benchmarking Random Search vs. Iterative Refinement for Parametric Icon Design
contents.abstract: Vision–language models (VLMs) can score images against natural-language intents. This capability makes "self-refining" design agents possible: a candidate is generated, feedback is produced, and the candidate is revised. For low-dimensional parametric graphics (e.g., facial icons), it is unclear whether this expensive loop beats a simple generate-and-filter workflow. We test VLM-guided parametric icon design under a strict budget of N=5 distinct candidates per run. Two strategies are compared: Random Top-k (sample N candidates and select the best) and a rule-based Self-refine loop (revise one candidate using VLM feedback). Using GPT-4o across four emotion tasks, Random Top-k matches or outperforms Self-refine (Joy +0.4, Sadness +4.4; ties on Anger/Neutral). We observe threshold-like score jumps rather than smooth gradients, which makes local refinement unreliable. These results suggest that for constrained parametric design, a lightweight generate-and-filter interface can be more robust and efficient than conversational iterative refinement.

contents.title: XAI Effect on Laypeople VS Experts Perceptions of AI Outcomes
contents.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly integrated into high-stakes domains that affect daily life. Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods are essential for understanding how these systems make decisions, particularly when using complex "black box" models. XAI methods are diverse, and we don't fully understand how different explanations work for different people in different contexts. Hence, this study presents preliminary results of work in process that aims to investigate the effect of different XAI methods (SHAP, LIME, and DiCE) with respect to different stakeholder types (laypeople vs. experts) and system domains (e.g., HR, Legal, Medical, Entertainment). This paper presents the initial results of our first case study, which focuses on an AI-based recruitment system.

contents.title: Qux360: A Validation Framework for Improving Reliability and Transparency of AI-Supported Qualitative Analysis
contents.abstract: Driven by fast-paced product cycles and advancements in generative technology, the use of generative AI (GenAI) for qualitative analysis has become increasingly common in user research. However, user research requires robust qualitative methodologies, and the integration of GenAI creates challenges in maintaining rigor. This work introduces Qux360, a framework that implements validation as a first-class concept in AI-assisted qualitative analysis, based on pain points identified in exploratory interviews with user researchers. We demo an application that showcases the analysis and validation capabilities of Qux360, including validators that check for different aspects of quality in automated participant identification, topic extraction, and thematic analysis. By facilitating transparent analysis without compromising efficiency, Qux360 enables researchers to deliver actionable results while maintaining high standards of research quality and integrity.

contents.title: Sensei: Intelligent Physiological Signal Acquisition and Analysis Dashboard
contents.abstract: Physiological signals are increasingly used to study stress, engagement, and user experience. Yet collecting and interpreting these signals remains difficult for students and novice researchers, who must navigate complex device setups, scattered software tools, and technical analysis workflows. This demo presents Sensei, an intelligent browser-based platform that streamlines the entire process of running small physiological studies—from connecting sensors and defining protocols to visualizing data and generating interpretations. In this demo, attendees will run a brief recording session and explore Sensei’s AI-assisted analysis tools, illustrating how intelligent support can make physiological computing more approachable for learning and early-stage research.

contents.title: Conversing with the Past: Immersive Access  to Cultural Digital Archives with LLMs and Spatial Interfaces
contents.abstract: Historical digitized archives, such as newspapers, preserve cultural memory but remain difficult to explore due to noisy OCR and rigid keyword-based search interfaces. We present a research demo that combines retrieval augmented generation with an immersive mixed reality interface on Apple Vision Pro, where interaction is driven entirely by voice and hand gestures. The system integrates LLM-based OCR refinement with a RAG pipeline over historical periodicals, enabling natural language queries, spatial browsing of results, and inspection of supporting evidence through timeline and geospatial visualizations. An empirical evaluation analyzes retrieval quality and system latency, illustrating how evidence-grounded information retrieval supports sense-making in large archival collections.

contents.title: MultEval: Collaboratively Creating Criteria for LLM-as-a-Judge Systems
contents.abstract: LLM-as-a-judge approaches have emerged as a scalable solution for evaluating model behaviors, yet they rely on evaluation criteria often created by a single individual, embedding that person's assumptions, priorities, and interpretive lens. In practice, defining such criteria is a collaborative and contested process involving multiple evaluators with different values, interpretations, and priorities—an aspect largely unsupported by existing tools. We present MultEval, a system designed to support collaborative criteria creation for LLM-as-a-judge workflows. MultEval enables multiple evaluators to jointly create, compare, and refine evaluation criteria by surfacing disagreements early, diagnosing sources of conflict using consensus-building theory, and encouraging structured exploration of alternative criteria formulations. Our system demonstrates how intelligent user interfaces can better support multi-stakeholder alignment in human–AI evaluation.

contents.title: DEMO: Enhancing Museum Accessibility through an Integrated Conversational AI Agent
contents.abstract: Ensuring accessible, meaningful, and personalized museum experiences for blind and low-vision (BLV) visitors remains a persistent challenge. This demo presents EMusA, an interactive tangible system that enables BLV visitors to explore 3D-printed replicas of museum artifacts while accessing contextual information through a dual-modality audio interface. EMusA integrates curator-approved, pre-recorded audio descriptions (AD) that are automatically triggered when a visitor lifts a physical replica, alongside an open-ended conversational artificial intelligence (AI) agent that can be queried on demand via a dedicated interaction button. Through interaction with EMusA, visitors can hold a replica and ask follow-up questions in their own words about the artifact, its context, and its cultural meaning, thereby supporting a more meaningful and personalized museum experience.

contents.title: Beyond data-centric explanations for mental healthcare professionals
contents.abstract: Mental healthcare is facing a capacity crisis where healthcare professionals (HP) do not find the time to interpret the ``data overload'' generated by longitudinal monitoring tools (wearables and ESM) and do not trust the black-box nature of AI-implemented tools. We developed a mental healthcare dashboard, designed through three iterative co-design cycles with HPs in a real-world setting, to bridge this gap. This dashboard synthesizes multiple data streams into a glanceable, summative overview featuring AI-driven stress risk predictions, context-aware recommendations via a RAG-LLM pipeline, and a source-verification mechanism to enable evidence-based reliability. By providing interpretable and interconnected panels, including an interactive assistant for source-grounded queries, our tool aims to reduce cognitive load and increase appropriate trust in this mental health dashboard. This demo highlights how intelligent interfaces can transform raw data into actionable and clinically informed therapeutic insights.

contents.title: Prompt to Press: Evaluating Human Perception of AI Involvement in News Writing Across Prompt Specificity
contents.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming a common feature in content creation tools, prompting important questions about how design choices influence user trust and engagement in AI-assisted journalism. Beyond output quality, factors such as prompt specificity, model choice, and authorship disclosure are themselves interaction design parameters that influence how users interpret and evaluate AI contributions. Yet, little is known about how these design decisions affect reader perceptions in journalistic contexts. To address this gap, we conducted an experiment with 150 participants who evaluated news articles on the sensitive topic of assisted suicide. The articles systematically varied in authorship (human-written, AI-edited, or AI-generated), stance (pro- or anti-legalization), and prompt specificity (vague, moderate, or highly detailed). Participants rated each article on engagement, subjectivity, and perceived AI involvement, and also provided open-ended justifications for their authorship judgments. Our findings show that prompt specificity and model choice significantly influence perceptions of authorship and credibility, underscoring how technical design decisions in AI tools can shape public trust in journalism

contents.title: “There is more potential for AI in vocational high-school”: Exploring perceptions on AI trust and control boundaries of vocational high-school teachers
contents.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded in everyday teaching workflows—from grading and feedback to personalization and engagement support. Yet educators’ willingness to adopt AI relies on their trust in tools and to what extends that allows the control to the teacher. This paper reports on an exploratory study – combining questionnaire and workshop - on the perspectives of 18 vocational high-school teachers on the use and potential of AI in Education, their concerns and challenges in allowing AI to control specific aspects of learning. Our findings show that teachers in vocational high-school see great potential in the use of AI for teaching and learning, while also raising their concerns with regards to the validity of outputs and overuse or misuse of such tools.

contents.title: How Many Kilometers Can I Drive Before Shisa-chan Gets Sick? Affective LLM-AR Gamification for Mobility Behavior Change in Okinawa
contents.abstract: Reducing CO2 emissions is often framed as a collective good, yet it remains a distant and abstract concept in the context of everyday life. This study seeks to translate these remote and hard-to-quantify emission reduction goals into easily comprehensible pet-nurturing actions. By gamifying affective interactions mediated through a mobile-based augmented reality (AR) agent powered by a large language model (LLM), we developed an embodied representation of Shisa, the guardian spirit of Okinawa Island. Through explicit representation of the island's transportation-related emissions using Shisa's emotions and advice, our preliminary study with stakeholders, including local residents and government representatives, revealed increased emission awareness (e.g., feelings of guilt) and social tensions that emerged when participants considered changing their transport modes (e.g., negotiation). We conclude with a reflection on the practical and societal impacts of our study and future research plans for large-scale empirical deployment.

contents.title: Making AI Risk Decisions Visible: Evaluating LLM Reasoning in Insurance Risk Classification
contents.abstract:
Accurate risk assessment is central to insurance underwriting, yet the growing use of Large Language Models (LLMs) introduces challenges around hallucination, uncertainty, and regulatory compliance. Emerging frameworks such as the EU AI Act require high-risk AI systems to provide transparent, traceable reasoning. In this work, we investigate how LLMs interpret insurance risk rules and propose a framework that quantifies and visualises the reliability of LLM reasoning to support human underwriters. Using a structured prompting strategy across multiple insurance products, we analyse token-level log-probabilities to compute four reasoning-quality metrics: entropy, average predictive probability, perplexity, and semantic energy. These metrics are integrated into an interactive user interface that highlights the strength, fragility, or uncertainty of model-generated explanations for each risk level. Through a case study with an insurance company, we demonstrate how reasoning-based re-labelling improves classification accuracy from 75% to 82%, reducing false medium- and high-risk assignments. Our results show that making LLM reasoning visible can enhance trust, support regulatory requirements, and enable more informed decision-making in high-stakes insurance settings.

Keywords: LLM reasoning, uncertainty quantification, explainable AI, insurance risk assessment, token-level analysis, human-AI collaboration.

contents.title: VERDICT: Visualized Evaluation and Reasoning with Dynamic IRAC-based Computational Thinking on Graph Structures
contents.abstract: Legal reasoning requires structured logical analysis, yet existing AI approaches suffer from opacity and limited verifiability. We present VERDICT, a hybrid system combining LLMs with a Neo4j legal knowledge graph (2,700+ nodes) to support transparent legal reasoning through interactive IRAC (Issue, Rule, Application, Conclusion) visualization. Our evaluation with 13 participants revealed expertise dependent perceptions: law students valued educational transparency (80% recommendation), while legal professionals prioritized accuracy concerns (50% recommendation). This divergence suggests that AI tools for professional reasoning domains may find initial value in education rather than direct practice, supporting human expertise development rather than replacement.

contents.title: Waste Warrior: Architecture and Design of a Gamified Mobile App for Healthcare
contents.abstract: The NHS faces significant challenges in achieving its net-zero carbon emissions targets, with waste management representing a critical area where staff encouragement could deliver substantial environmental and cost benefits. Informed by baseline research n=55 revealing high motivation Mean (M)=4.3 but constrained by time pressure M=3.2 and knowledge gaps M=3.4, this work presents a gamified mobile application with three technical contributions: (1) sub-second WebSocket feedback for habit formation at point of disposal, (2) low-friction classification interface with contextual item filtering reducing cognitive load and, (3) mastery-based educational system addressing recycling misconceptions. The system is implemented using Laravel (22 API endpoints) and Flutter (cross-platform client), validated through 226 automated tests. This work demonstrates technical feasibility of real-time behaviour change applications for time-constrained healthcare environments, establishing a foundation for pilot deployment in NHS trust settings.
